parents.father_research_notes: Wales family of Westhampton MA. Specific parents not identified.
Wales family of Westhampton MA. Specific parents not identified.

military_service.war: Likely War of 1812 (age 26 at outbreak; rank Capt confirmed)
military_service.notes: Findagrave Memorial #122605184 carries 'VETERAN' marker. Captain rank confirmed in displayed name. Born 1786 — would have been 26 at outbreak of War of 1812. Specific unit + service dates not yet documented.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at seventeen, first child at eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: Married Sylvia Wales 1803 in Westhampton when both were seventeen years old. Their first child Emily Hooker Kingsley was born May 1804 when Henry was eighteen. Five children followed over seventeen years, the last (Almira Hooker, born 1820) carrying the name of Henry's young-deceased sister Almira Hooker Hale who had died age twenty-four in 1816.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband died on daughter Ellen's thirty-fourth birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Orrin Kingsley died June 6, 1876 at Westhampton — the thirty-fourth birthday of his youngest daughter Ellen Maria, born June 6, 1842 at Northampton. Emily had predeceased him by five years, dying April 17, 1871 at Westhampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Named son Charles Hooker, preserving birth surname
narrative_blocks.body: Among her six Kingsley children, Emily gave the fourth the middle name Hooker — placing her birth surname in the middle position so the family identity would persist into the next Kingsley generation. The child died young; the Hooker name did not carry forward in the surviving siblings.

narrative_blocks.header: Adopted Boston-born Louisa, who died at three
narrative_blocks.body: Emily and Orrin Kingsley adopted a Boston-born child, Louisa W. Kingsley, who came to Westhampton sometime before November 1847. She died there three years after her 1844 birth; her original family surname appears as Somerby on FindAGrave records.

parents.father_research_notes: Ezra Kingsley of Northampton MA per EH 1909 #2748.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #2748: 'He was a grocer at Northampton, Mass.'

narrative_blocks.header: Three infants lost in four years, 1848-1852
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Ann and Oliver Bridgeman lost three children in succession at Northampton: Louisa died at three months in 1848, her replacement-named sister Louisa died at sixteen months in 1852, and an unnamed son died at nineteen days in August 1852. Three bereavements in four years, before any child survived to adulthood.

narrative_blocks.header: Died thirty-three days after childbirth, aged thirty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Henry died February 3, 1861 at Westhampton — thirty-three days after giving birth to George Henry on January 1 — at age thirty-three, the same number in years as the days she survived her last delivery. Her daughter Mary Hooker was three years old; George Henry never knew his mother.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Henry likely honors maternal grandfather Capt. Henry Hooker of Westhampton MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Died fifteen days after Emily Hooker's birth, 1855
narrative_blocks.body: Emily Elsie gave birth to her second daughter April 2, 1855 at Westhampton — naming her Emily Hooker, preserving both her own first name and her Hooker birth surname. She died fifteen days later on April 17, 1855 at twenty-four, leaving two young children and a farmer husband.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Hooker carries mother Emily Hooker Kingsley's birth surname into the Kingsley line.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #4344: 'He was a freight agent.'

narrative_blocks.header: Died young; Ellen Munson was six months old
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Munson Kingsley died August 12, 1868 at Williamsburg MA at twenty-nine — leaving wife Josephine and two young daughters, Carrie Elsie not yet two and Ellen Munson born only six and a half months earlier. He had already lost his first child Charles Hooker to infant death in 1865.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Detroit physician; widowed at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Ellen Maria married Dr. Judson Bradley in 1873 and followed him to Detroit, where he practiced medicine until his death June 14, 1889 at fifty-three — eight days after Ellen's forty-seventh birthday. He was buried at Westhampton, where her parents had lived and died.

narrative_blocks.header: The only adopted child in this Hooker branch
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa W. Kingsley was born in Boston in November 1844 and adopted by Oren and Emily Hooker Kingsley of Westhampton — the only adopted child documented in this branch. She died at Westhampton three years later in November 1847; her birth family name appears as Somerby on FindAGrave records.

bio.bio_blurb: Westhampton farmer of the Hooker line

narrative_blocks.header: Buried infant Worthington; named next son Worthington
narrative_blocks.body: Festus named his fifth child Worthington in April 1846, but the infant died December 10 of that year at eight months. His sixth son, born November 1848, was named Worthington again — the replacement naming common in the era, and a pattern repeated in the same Hooker generation when his uncle Henry named a daughter Almira after a deceased sibling.

narrative_blocks.header: He gave the lost son’s name again
narrative_blocks.body: When his infant son Worthington died, the Westhampton farmer gave the name again to his next boy — a quiet act of remembrance carried in the family register.

parents.father_research_notes: Chester Strong of Huntington MA per EH 1909 #2749.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Thompson may honor great-grandmother Anna Thompson Hooker, mother of Dr. William Hooker.
First name Henry honors maternal great-grandfather Capt. Henry Hooker of Westhampton MA.
Middle name Chester honors maternal great-grandfather Chester Strong of Huntington MA.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #4348: 'He was a farmer at Maple Creek, Nebraska.'

narrative_blocks.header: Farmer who left Westhampton for Nebraska frontier
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Wales Hooker and Jane Clark settled at Maple Creek in Custer County Nebraska — part of the Yankee wave that pushed the Hooker family's agricultural tradition onto the Great Plains. Five of their six children were born in Nebraska; Charles Strong lived only five days there in 1878 and Anna Worthington had died earlier at Fremont at age two.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Wales honors paternal grandmother Sylvia Wales Hooker of Westhampton MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Son George Hooker Knight carried Hooker surname forward
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Ashmun gave her only documented child the middle name Hooker — preserving her birth surname in the Knight line, as other women in this Hooker generation had done in the Drury and Bartlett lines. Her husband George was the Westhampton librarian; his mother's birth surname was Kingsley, connecting the Knight family to the broader Hampshire County network that surrounded this branch.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for paternal aunt Lucy Ashmun Hooker Clark (H02753), who died April 17, 1839 at Rushville IL at age twenty-six.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson of Mary Strong married another Strong, 1905
narrative_blocks.body: Harold Worthington Hooker married Rubie Mae Strong on October 12, 1905 — repeating the family's connection to the Strong family that began when his grandfather Festus Hooker married Mary Strong of Huntington in 1832. Whether Rubie Mae was related to the Huntington Strongs is not documented in EH.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Worthington to replace his infant brother Worthington 1st (H04350), who died December 10, 1846 at eight months.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #4352: 'He is a farmer at Westhampton, Mass.'

narrative_blocks.header: Named Anson Hooker, he survived just nine days
narrative_blocks.body: Electa and Anson Chapman's third child was given the name Anson Hooker Chapman — combining the father's first name with the mother's birth surname. He was born July 14, 1838 and buried July 23, nine days later; the Hooker surname did not survive in the Chapman line.

parents.father_research_notes: Jedediah Chapman of Westhampton MA per EH 1909 #2750.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #2750: 'He was a merchant at Westhampton.'

narrative_blocks.header: Millwright husband worked Puget Sound, then New Hampshire
narrative_blocks.body: Maryett's husband Edward Webster Kingsley made a remarkable career arc — millwright at Puget Sound in Washington Territory, then lumber manufacturer at Coos New Hampshire, before settling back at Westhampton as a mechanic. The couple had one documented child, Edward Anson Kingsley, born 1878.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband by ten years; nearly ninety
narrative_blocks.body: Maryett Chapman Kingsley outlived her husband Edward Webster Kingsley by ten years — he died at Westhampton July 6, 1913, she on April 29, 1923 at age eighty-nine. Her life ran from the Jackson presidency to the early 1920s, rooted in the same Westhampton community for over half a century.

naming_inspiration.description: First name Anson honors father Anson Chapman.
Middle name Hooker carries mother Electa Wales Hooker Chapman's birth surname into the Chapman line.
Named for her father Henry's sister Almira Hooker Hale, who died at Westhampton in 1816 at age twenty-three, four years before this Almira's birth.

burialCemetery.name: Center Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Westhampton MA. Primary burial ground for the William Hooker / Henry Hooker / Kingsley / Drury / Bartlett branch. FindAGrave Cemetery #2198549.
